g.title: Electronic Display Screen Component
g.desc: This is one rectangular electronic display screen. It is a thin, flat piece, likely made of smooth plastic or glass on the front, and plastic or metal on the back. Run your fingers around all edges; on one of the shorter sides, you will feel a distinct, small, rectangular protrusion. This helps you identify and orient the display correctly.

g.title: Speaker Module and Audio Cable
g.desc: This is your speaker module. It's a small, rectangular plastic box, not made of wood like many other parts. On one flat side, feel for a slightly raised circular area – that's the speaker grille. A flexible, thin cable comes out from one end of the box. Follow this cable to find a small, smooth, metal plug, like a headphone jack, at its end. To orient it, place the speaker module with the circular speaker grille facing up.

g.title: Top Panel for Display and Controls
g.desc: Find a large, flat wooden panel, about 5mm thick. Feel its outer edge, which has many small, rectangular bumps and dips all the way around, like castle battlements. In the middle of the panel, you'll find two main openings. The larger one is a big rectangle on the left side, meant for a screen. To the right of this large opening, you'll feel five small, separate rectangular cutouts that spell out the word 'PIPER'. Below these letters and to the right of the large screen opening, there is another open area. This area has a straight bottom, a straight top, but its right edge is smoothly curved, especially at the top-right corner. There are also several small circular screw holes: two near the top edge, one near the bottom edge, one on the left edge, and one inside the curved open area on the right.

g.title: Piper Screen Back Panel
g.desc: This is a large, flat, rectangular piece of 5mm thick plywood, which is the back panel for your computer screen. Feel along its entire outer edge; it has a repeating pattern of rectangular cut-outs, like the top of a castle wall, all around. There are four small, round screw holes, one centered on each of the four sides, located within the flatter sections of the notched edge. In the very middle of this panel, you will find an etched design that spells out the word 'PIPER' in individual block letters, with wavy lines connecting them, resembling small cables.

g.title: Brass #4-40 Hex Nuts
g.desc: This component is a small, brass #4-40 hex nut. You will need 22 of these. By touch, identify them as tiny, six-sided metal pieces with a threaded hole in the center. They are smooth and slightly heavy for their size, and no specific orientation is required.

g.title: Kit Battery Overview
g.desc: This is the single main battery for your kit. Tactilely, it feels like a smooth, solid rectangle made of plastic. Its two shorter ends are smoothly rounded, while its two longer sides and its top and bottom surfaces are flat. It should feel noticeably denser and heavier than other plastic components of a similar size. Look for a cable or a charging port often located on one of the ends or a long side.

g.title: Component A: Half-inch Screws
g.desc: Locate the small, metallic hardware pieces. These are brass screws, which will feel smooth and slightly warm to the touch compared to plastic. Feel along the length of a screw. One end will have a slightly wider, flatter head, usually with a slot or a cross-shaped indentation to fit a screwdriver. The other end, the shaft, will be cylindrical and covered in fine, raised spiral ridges, called threads. To identify these as the 1/2 inch screws (Component A), measure their total length from the tip of the threads to just under the screw head; it should be exactly half an inch. You will need 26 of these screws.

g.title: Raspberry Pi Board Component
g.desc: This is one Raspberry Pi circuit board, a small, flat, rectangular electronic component. Run your fingers along its edges: one long side has multiple rectangular openings for USB ports, and next to them, a slightly wider rectangular opening for an Ethernet port. The opposite long side features a prominent row of many small, upright pins, feeling like a tiny comb or fence. One of the short ends will have a small, round audio jack and a power port (which may feel like a small oval or a flat rectangular slot). On the top surface, you can feel several small, raised rectangular chips and potentially flat ribbon cable connectors.

g.title: Piece 1-3: Left Side Screen Frame
g.desc: This long wood piece is labeled 1-3. To identify and orient it, place it horizontally so that a row of five small square notches is along the top edge. On the far left vertical end, there is a T-shaped cutout. There is also a T-shaped cutout on the top edge just to the right of the left corner. Along the bottom edge, you will feel a large rectangular gap followed by a third T-shaped cutout. The right end of the piece is distinct, with the bottom edge curving smoothly upward to meet the top corner.

g.title: Component 1-5: Long Contoured Side Plate
g.desc: This is a long, flat wooden piece labeled with the number '1-5'. It features a square left end and a decorative, wave-like curve on the right end. Along the top and bottom edges, you will feel several small, rectangular tabs. There are three T-shaped notches: one on the flat vertical edge of the left side, one on the top edge, and one on the bottom edge. A small circular hole is located near the tip of the curved right end.

g.title: Screen Top Front Panel
g.desc: This is a long, flat 5mm thick laser-cut plywood piece, serving as the front panel for your computer screen. Tactilely, find its distinctive shape: both the top and bottom long edges have a repeating pattern of wide, shallow notches and tabs, similar to a castle wall. On the left end, locate a large circular cutout with the number '1-4' lightly etched inside, next to a small round screw hole. On the right end, you'll find a small round screw hole, followed by another large circular cutout with '1-4' etched inside. In the central area of the piece, feel for two small round holes near the top edge, and two T-shaped cutouts that extend downwards from the top edge.

g.title: Long Screen Support Piece Label 1-6
g.desc: A very long and narrow plywood piece. Both of its long edges have many rectangular tabs sticking out like teeth. Each of the two short ends has a T-shaped notch cut into it. There are also two more T-shaped notches cut into the main body of the piece, pointing in opposite directions. Look for the number 1-6 engraved in a small circle near one end.

g.title: Battery Charger and Cable
g.desc: This entry shows the battery charger and its connecting cable, both made of plastic and flexible wire. The charger is a solid, rectangular plastic block, roughly the size of a small soap bar, about 1-2 inches on its longest side. On one of its faces, you will feel two flat metal prongs that stick out, which are used to plug it into a wall outlet. On another face, there's a single rectangular hole, which is the USB port. The cable is a flexible, black wire, usually about 2-3 feet long. At one end, feel for a flat, rectangular plastic connector, which is a USB-A type. It has a thin, flat metal part inside that helps you feel which way to plug it into the charger. The other end of the cable has a smaller, differently shaped plastic connector, like a tiny rectangle or a trapezoid, such as a micro-USB or USB-C. This end plugs into the battery or electronic module. Always feel the unique shape and orientation of both connectors carefully before plugging them in.

g.title: Identify Four 3/8-inch Brass Screws (B)
g.desc: You are looking for four small brass screws, labeled 'B' on your blueprint. Feel their smooth, metallic surface. Each screw measures 3/8 inches long; you can measure from its pointed end to the underside of its rounded, domed head. The shaft has distinct, spiraling threads. The head will likely have a cross-shaped indentation for a screwdriver.

g.title: Identify 1/4 inch brass screws
g.desc: Find four small brass screws. These screws are approximately 1/4 inch long from the underside of their flat, round heads to the tip of their shafts. You can identify them by feeling the smooth, cool metal of the brass and the fine, spiraling ridges, called threads, on their shafts. The heads are flat and slightly wider than the threaded shaft.

g.title: Four Plastic Retainer Clips
g.desc: Find four small plastic pieces. Unlike the wooden parts, these are smooth and feel like hard plastic. Each clip has a solid, rectangular base. From one side of this base, a curved arm extends upwards and then curls over, creating an open hook shape. On the front, flat face of the rectangular base, you will feel a small, round hole that goes completely through.

g.title: Electronic Components: LEDs
g.desc: Locate the small, usually clear or colored plastic components. Each LED has a rounded or dome-shaped top and two thin metal wires, called 'leads,' extending from the flatter bottom. To identify polarity for correct orientation, carefully feel for one lead that is slightly longer than the other, or for a flat edge on the plastic body near the leads.

g.title: Kit Components: Six Brass Standoffs
g.desc: Locate six identical, small, cylindrical brass hardware pieces. With your fingers, you'll feel one end has external threads, like a small screw. The other end has a smooth, internally threaded opening, designed for a screw to fasten into.

g.title: Electronic Buttons for Your Kit
g.desc: Locate two small electronic buttons. Each button has a flat, square base. From the center of this base, a round, smooth cylinder rises. The very top surface of this cylinder is the part you press, and you should be able to feel it give slightly when pushed. These buttons are usually made of plastic.

g.title: Left and Right Wood Gear Latches
g.desc: These two 5mm plywood pieces are mirror images of each other. Each features a small central pivot hole and a distinct hook shape at the bottom. To identify them, hold a piece with the hook at the bottom opening toward the center. The Left Latch has a smooth, rounded curve on its left edge and jagged gear teeth on its right edge. The Right Latch has the smooth curve on its right edge and gear teeth on its left edge. These teeth are designed to interlock when the pieces are placed side-by-side.

g.title: 200 mm Jumper Wires
g.desc: This drawing represents a bundle of six individual jumper wires. Each wire is 200 millimeters long. To identify them, feel for a group of thin, flexible wires, often joined together like a flat ribbon. At both ends of these wires, you will find small plastic connectors that feel like tiny rectangular blocks with six individual openings.

g.title: Computer Kit Base Plate
g.desc: This is the largest and flattest plywood piece, about 5mm thick. To identify it, feel for its substantial size and smooth surface. Its outer edge is not straight; it has many unique 'teeth' and 'notches' for connecting to other parts. Feel along the top and bottom edges for prominent T-shaped cutouts that stick out. The left and right edges have a series of smaller, repeating rectangular notches. On the flat surface, you will find several openings: a long, narrow vertical rectangular slot near the top left, and two shorter, wider horizontal rectangular slots positioned towards the bottom center. There are also several small circular holes, uniform in size, spread across the plate for fastening hardware.

g.title: Base Back Panel Part 2-2
g.desc: This is a long, flat piece of 5mm thick plywood, like a thick wooden ruler but wider. Find the etched numbers '2-2' on its surface to confirm this is the correct piece. To orient it, feel for the corners: two opposite corners are smoothly rounded (top-right and bottom-left when oriented correctly), while the other two corners are sharp right angles. Hold the piece so its long edges are vertical. Feel the top edge: it has a small rectangular notch in the center, flanked by two T-shaped notches. Now feel the bottom edge: it also has a small rectangular notch in the center, but the two notches on either side are wider and shaped like a mushroom or keyhole, which helps distinguish the bottom. On the long left edge, you'll find three rectangular cutouts, evenly spaced. On the long right edge, there are two smaller rectangular cutouts (one near the top and one near the bottom), a larger rectangular cutout with rounded corners in the middle, and another T-shaped notch located between the large middle cutout and the bottom small rectangular cutout.

g.title: Toolbox Side Wood Piece 2-3
g.desc: A flat rectangular wood piece with two wide feet on the bottom edge and a rounded arch on the top edge. A large circular hole is cut into the center of the arch. Below that circle, you'll find a small round hole and a small rectangular cutout. One of the shorter sides contains a tall, narrow vertical slot, while the bottom center between the feet features a T-shaped notch.

g.title: Toolbox Back Component 2-4
g.desc: A long, rectangular piece of 5mm thick laser-cut plywood. Its top and bottom long edges are straight and smooth. On both of the shorter left and right sides, you will find a small rectangular tab sticking out from the middle. Just above each tab on these short sides, there is a T-shaped notch cut into the wood. To orient it, lay the piece horizontally so the tabs and notches are on the left and right ends.

g.title: Toolbox Front Panel, Plastic Piece 2-5
g.desc: This is a flat, rectangular piece of laser-cut plastic. You can identify it by feeling for four tabs extending from its edges: one small, square-like tab centered on each of the short ends (left and right), and one wider, rounded tab centered on each of the long edges (top and bottom). The piece is symmetrical, so its orientation is flexible.

g.title: Plastic Toolbox Lid (Component 2-6)
g.desc: This piece is a wide, rectangular laser-cut plastic lid. You can identify the top edge by the small tabs protruding from each upper corner. The bottom edge features a central section that curves outward, containing a small, horizontal rectangular slot. This slot is designed as a finger pull for lifting the lid once installed.

g.title: Identify Two Kit Breadboards
g.desc: Find two rectangular plastic breadboards from your kit. Both pieces are flat and have many small, circular holes arranged in a grid pattern on their top surface that you can feel with your fingers. One breadboard is noticeably longer than the other. The holes are typically arranged in rows that run along the length of each board, which can help you orient them.

g.title: Right Base Panel (Part 2-7)
g.desc: This is a long, flat 5mm thick plywood piece, shaped like a tall rectangle. Find the four large circular holes located at each of its four corners. The left edge is mostly straight and runs between its top and bottom corner holes. Along this left edge, feel four vertical rectangular slots alternating with three small circular holes. The right edge is wavy, curving outward in the middle, and also connects its top and bottom corner holes. In the upper-middle of the piece, there's a small rectangular slot and a small circular hole. Below that, towards the bottom-center, find a horizontal rectangular slot. The number '2-7' is etched into the wood near the top-left and bottom-left areas.

g.title: Computer Base Left Side Panel 3-1
g.desc: This is a flat, 5mm thick plywood piece for the computer base. To identify and orient it, run your fingers along the edges. The long left edge is perfectly straight. The top edge curves gently from the top left corner, then drops sharply down the right side. The long right edge is mostly straight, but about one-third of the way up from the bottom, you'll feel a subtle inward notch. The bottom edge is mostly straight, curving up to meet a large circular hole at the bottom right. There is another large circular hole at the top left. Along the straight left edge, feel four narrow rectangular slots alternating with four small circular holes, starting with a rectangular slot at the top. In the center-right, there is a large vertical rectangular cutout. Near the bottom-left corner, you'll find a small rectangular slot, marked with an engraved '3-1', and a small circular hole immediately to its right.

g.title: Computer Base Front Panel
g.desc: This long, flat plywood piece is 5mm thick and is the front panel for your computer's base. You can identify it by its elongated rectangular shape. To orient it, feel for the bottom edge, which has two deep corner notches and a U-shaped dip in its center. The top edge has two shallower corner notches. Along one of the long sides, about a quarter of the way down from the top, you'll feel two small, round screw holes for brass hardware. Just below these holes, on the same side, is a gentle, curved indentation. This side with the holes and curve is the front.

g.title: Y-Split USB Power Harness Cable
g.desc: Locate the single Y-split power harness cable. Tactilely, you'll find one larger, rectangular USB-A connector at one end. This connector has a smooth top surface and a slightly recessed opening on the bottom for its pins, ensuring it only plugs in one way. Follow the flexible cable from this USB-A end. You'll feel it split into two separate, thinner cables. At the end of each of these two split cables, identify a smaller, rounded rectangular (pill-shaped) USB-C connector. These USB-C connectors are symmetrical and can be inserted either way up.

g.title: 3.5mm Audio Cable with Angled Jack
g.desc: This is a flexible, black audio cable with two different 3.5mm jack connectors. One connector is straight: feel for a metal cylinder with small raised rings near its pointed tip, where the cable enters directly into its base. The other connector is a right-angle jack: feel how the cable bends into a small plastic housing, and the metal jack extends out at a 90-degree angle from that housing, also with raised rings near its tip. Both jacks are the same size and will fit into standard 3.5mm audio ports.

g.title: HDMI Cable for Display
g.desc: Locate one long, flexible cable, usually black. At each end, you will find an identical rectangular plastic connector, about an inch long. Feel for the wider, flat side on the connector, and on the opposite side, notice a distinct, flattened edge or a slight indentation. This unique shape helps you orient the connector to plug it in correctly.

g.title: Switches & Buzzers
g.desc: This section displays three small electronic components for your kit. First, a buzzer that feels like a smooth, round cylinder, about the size of a small button, with two stiff wire legs sticking straight down from its flat bottom. Next, a push-button switch, which is a small square block with a round, clickable button on its top surface. It has four thin wire legs underneath, one at each corner. Finally, a slide switch, which is a small, rectangular block with a movable lever or bar on its top surface that you can slide back and forth, and several wire legs underneath arranged in a line.

g.title: Title and Header
g.desc: Title and Header
